---
title: "Wholesale_customer_segmentation"
output: html_document
date: "2026-04-08"
---

Importing Dataset
```{r}
library(dplyr)
library(cluster)
whole = read.csv("C://Users//amogh//OneDrive//Documents//MSBA//Introduction to Business Analytics//HW1//Wholesale customers data.csv")
head(whole)
```

Normalizing the Data
```{r}
normalize = function(x){
return ((x - min(x))/(max(x) - min(x)))}
whole_normalize = whole %>% mutate(across(3:8, normalize))
head(whole_normalize)
```

Calculating Distance Matrix
```{r}
distance_matrix = dist(whole_normalize[,3:8], method = "euclidean") 
```

Hierarchical Clustering
```{r}
hierarchical = hclust(distance_matrix, method = "ward.D")
plot(hierarchical, labels = whole_normalize$Channel)
rect.hclust(hierarchical, k = 3)
```

K-Means Clustering
```{r}
set.seed(123)
kcluster <- kmeans(whole_normalize[, 3:8], centers = 3, nstart = 25)
kcluster$centers
```

```{r}
whole_normalize$kcluster = kcluster$cluster
whole$kcluster = kcluster$cluster
cluster_sizes_k = table(whole$kcluster)
cluster_sizes_k
```

Confirming k-value
```{r}
library(cluster)
SSE_curve <- c()
for (n in 1:10) {
kcluster = kmeans(whole_normalize[,3:8], n)
sse = kcluster$tot.withinss
SSE_curve[n] = sse}
# plot SSE against number of clusters
plot(1:10, SSE_curve, type = "b")

#inspection- elbow point
diff(SSE_curve)
# Compute differences in SSE between consecutive cluster counts
sse_diff <- diff(SSE_curve)
# Plot the differences
plot(2:10, sse_diff, type = "b", pch = 19, col = "blue",
xlab = "Number of Clusters (k)",
ylab = "Change in SSE",
main = "SSE Drop Between Cluster Counts")
abline(h = 0, lty = 2, col = "gray")

#inspection- silhoutte score
for (k in 3:4) {
kcluster_test = kmeans(whole_normalize[,3:8], centers = k)
sc = silhouette(kcluster_test$cluster, dist(whole_normalize[,3:8]))
cat("k =", k, " | Silhouette Score =", mean(sc[, 3]), "\n")
}
```

Cluster Visualization & Post Hoc Analysis
```{r}
library(tidyr)
library(scales)
library(ggplot2)
whole$Channel = whole$Channel
whole$Region = whole$Region
whole <- whole %>%
  mutate(cluster_name = case_when(
    # R's K-Means usually starts at 1, 2, 3
    kcluster == 1 ~ "Low Spending Horeca",
    kcluster == 2 ~ "High Spending Horeca",
    kcluster == 3 ~ "Retail",
    TRUE ~ as.character(kcluster) 
  ))
# Set the factor levels for consistent plotting order
whole$cluster_name = factor(
    whole$cluster_name,
    levels = c("Retail", "High Spending Horeca", "Low Spending Horeca") 
)

# Data Processing
cluster_spending_means = whole %>%
  group_by(cluster_name) %>%
  summarise(
    Fresh = mean(Fresh),
    Milk = mean(Milk),
    Grocery = mean(Grocery),
    Frozen = mean(Frozen),
    Detergents_Paper = mean(Detergents_Paper),
    Delicatessen = mean(Delicatessen),
    Total_Mean_Spending = sum(Fresh, Milk, Grocery, Frozen, Detergents_Paper, Delicatessen)
  )
cluster_spending_long = cluster_spending_means %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(Fresh, Milk, Grocery, Frozen, Detergents_Paper, Delicatessen),
    names_to = "Product_Category",
    values_to = "Mean_Spending"
  )
# Plot the total mean spending for each cluster
ggplot(cluster_spending_means, aes(x = cluster_name, y = Total_Mean_Spending, fill = cluster_name)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", color = "black", alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(
    title = "Total Mean Annual Spending by Cluster",
x = "Cluster",
y = "Total Mean Annual Spending (USD)",
fill = "Cluster"
)
# Plot spending of clusters in each product category
ggplot(cluster_spending_long, aes(x = Product_Category, y = Mean_Spending, fill = cluster_name)) +
geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "dodge", color = "black", alpha = 0.8) +
scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) + # Use scales::comma for readable large numbers
labs(
title = "Mean Annual Spending by Product Category (K-Means Clusters)",
subtitle = "Cluster Profile: Identifying the largest purchase categories per segment.",
x = "Product Category",
y = "Mean Annual Spending (USD)",
fill = "Cluster"
)

# Prepare categorical data with clear labels
whole_labeled = whole %>%
mutate(
# Channel: 1 = Horeca (Hotel/Restaurant/Cafe), 2 = Retail
Channel_Label = factor(Channel, levels = c(1, 2), labels = c("Horeca", "Retail")),
# Region: Assuming 1=Lisbon, 2=Oporto, 3=Other/Unknown based on standard dataset info
Region_Label = factor(Region, levels = c(1, 2, 3), labels = c("Lisbon", "Oporto", "Other/Unk
nown"))
)
# Plot proportion of channel in each cluster
# By grouping by 'cluster_name', the column is kept for the plot
channel_plot = whole_labeled %>%
group_by(cluster_name, Channel_Label) %>% # SOLUTION: Group by the name directly
summarise(Count = n(), .groups = 'drop') %>%
group_by(cluster_name) %>% # Also use the name here for proportion calculation
mutate(Proportion = Count / sum(Count)) %>%
ggplot(aes(x = cluster_name, y = Proportion, fill = Channel_Label)) +
geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "fill", color = "black") +
scale_y_continuous(labels = percent) +
labs(
title = "Sales Channel Distribution within K-Means Clusters",
x = "Customer Segment",
y = "Proportion of Customers",
fill = "Sales Channel"
)
# Plot regional distribution in each cluster
region_plot = whole_labeled %>%
ggplot(aes(x = cluster_name, fill = Region_Label)) +
geom_bar(position = "dodge", color = "black") +
labs(
title = "Customer Count by Region within K-Means Clusters",
subtitle = "Geographic Concentration: Absolute count of customers per region.",
x = "Cluster",
y = "Number of Customers",
fill = "Region"
)

# PCA Scatter Plot with Ellipses
pca_result <- prcomp(whole_normalize[, 3:8], scale. = TRUE)
# Combine the PCA results (first two components) with your final cluster names
pca_data <- as.data.frame(pca_result$x[, 1:2])
pca_data$cluster_name <- whole$cluster_name # Use final names from 'whole' dataframe
# Extract the variance explained by the first two components for the plot labels
pca_summary <- summary(pca_result)
variance_explained <- pca_summary$importance[2, 1:2]
# Create the PCA plot
pca_plot <- ggplot(pca_data, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = cluster_name)) +
geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.6) +
stat_ellipse(geom = "polygon", alpha = 0.2, aes(fill = cluster_name)) +
labs(
title = "Customer Segments Visualized with PCA (k=3)",
subtitle = paste0("PC1 and PC2 together explain ", round(sum(variance_explained) * 100, 1), 
"% of the variance."),
x = paste0("Principal Component 1 (", round(variance_explained[1] * 100, 1), "%)"),
y = paste0("Principal Component 2 (", round(variance_explained[2] * 100, 1), "%)"),
color = "Customer Segment",
fill = "Customer Segment"
) +
# ADD THESE LINES TO ADJUST AXIS LIMITS (Zoom out)
xlim(min(pca_data$PC1) * 1.2, max(pca_data$PC1) * 1.2) + # Extend 20% on each side
ylim(min(pca_data$PC2) * 1.2, max(pca_data$PC2) * 1.2) + # Extend 20% on each side
theme_minimal() +
theme(legend.position = "bottom")
# Print the new PCA plot
print(pca_plot)
```
